planting.note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist medium, or air-layer branches; fiddle-leaf fig is sensitive to being moved — choose a permanent location with bright, indirect light and consistent conditions. Direct sun causes leaf scorch, deep shade causes poor growth and dropping. Water when the top 2 inches of soil are dry and ensure good drainage to prevent root rot.

directions: Grow in bright, indirect light indoors or in filtered shade outdoors in zones 10–12; fiddle-leaf fig has become one of the most widely grown and immediately recognizable indoor trees of the early 21st century, prized for its enormous, deeply wavy-edged, violin-shaped leaves (12–18 inches long) in rich, dark, glossy green that create an unmistakably tropical and architecturally dramatic statement in interior spaces. Native to the lowland tropical rainforests of West Africa, it grows as a large tree to 50 feet in its native habitat. As a houseplant it requires bright, indirect light (a few feet from a south or west window), consistent watering (allowing the top 2 inches to dry between waterings), and avoidance of both overwatering (the primary cause of root rot and leaf drop) and cold drafts. It dislikes being moved and may drop leaves in response to environmental changes. In zones 10–12 it can be grown outdoors as a specimen tree in partial shade. Cleaning the large leaves with a damp cloth maintains their glossy appearance and maximizes light absorption.

planting.note: Sow seeds in spring or divide established clumps in spring or fall; spotted bellflower spreads by underground rhizomes and can be aggressive — choose a site where some spread is welcome or confine with a root barrier. It is notably heat-tolerant among bellflowers and performs well in the warmer parts of its zone range.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; spotted bellflower is a Japanese and Korean native that produces some of the largest, most unusual flowers in the bellflower genus — long (2 inches), drooping, tubular bells in cream to soft pink, each distinctly spotted and freckled inside with red-purple dots, hung in nodding clusters on plants only 12–18 inches tall. The interior spotting, only visible by looking up into the pendant flower, is exquisite and gives the flowers a quality more reminiscent of foxglove or digitalis than of typical bellflowers. It blooms in early summer and spreads vigorously by underground rhizomes to form colonies in semi-shaded woodland conditions. Several cultivars and hybrids are available including 'Cherry Bells' (deep rose-pink) and 'Bowl of Cherries.' The rhizomatous spread, while vigorous, makes it an effective groundcover under shrubs and trees. Somewhat more heat-tolerant than most temperate bellflowers, succeeding in the warmer zones of its range.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; climbing gloxinia grows rapidly and begins blooming in summer. It climbs by twining leaf petioles and needs a trellis, mesh, or shrub for support. In zones 9–10 it may overwinter as a perennial; in colder climates grow as an annual from seed or overwinter cutting-rooted plants indoors.

directions: Start seeds early indoors and train on a trellis or other support in full sun to part shade; climbing gloxinia is a Mexican native vine producing large (2–3 inch), softly tubular, trumpet-shaped flowers in clear, warm rose-pink to soft magenta — a flower form and color closely resembling gloxinia (Sinningia), hence the common name, on a freely climbing vine 6–10 feet long. The hairy, somewhat gloxinia-like leaves contribute to the resemblance. The flowers are abundant from summer through fall and the whole plant has a pleasant, cottage-garden quality — softly colored, generous with bloom, and adaptable to growing up fences, trellises, tripods, and into shrubs. Previously classified as Asarina erubescens, it belongs to the snapdragon family and shares some visual qualities with that family. 'Magic Dragon' is a widely available seed strain in the nursery trade. An excellent companion to softer-colored annual and tender perennial vines on a mixed-planting trellis or fence.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or plant container-grown plants in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in part shade; unlike most fuchsias, F. triphylla tolerates more heat and direct sun than hybrid fuchsias. It is damaged below 28°F. The cultivar 'Gartenmeister Bonstedt' (often simply sold as 'Gartenmeister') is the most widely available selection.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; honeysuckle fuchsia is a Caribbean species from Hispaniola that differs from the hybrid fuchsias in producing long, narrow, trumpet-like flowers in intense orange-red to salmon-red — clustered in pendant bunches at the branch tips — rather than the two-toned, skirted, pendant bells of typical hybrid fuchsias. The flowers are pure nectar-source flowers, highly specialized for hummingbird pollination in their native Caribbean habitat and irresistible to hummingbirds in cultivation. The cultivar 'Gartenmeister Bonstedt' has particularly rich orange-coral flowers with attractive bronze-tinted foliage and is far more heat-tolerant than most fuchsias — thriving in warm summers that would collapse conventional fuchsia hybrids. This heat tolerance, combined with hummingbird-attracting tubular flowers in summer and fall, makes it one of the most valuable warm-climate container plants. Often treated as an annual in zones below 9.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, fertile, well-drained, humus-rich soil in part shade; hardy fuchsia dies back to the ground in cold winters in zones 6–7 but resprouts vigorously from established roots in spring. In zones 8–9 it remains semi-evergreen to evergreen. Mulch the roots heavily in winter in zones 6–7.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, humus-rich soil in part shade; hardy fuchsia is the most cold-tolerant fuchsia species — a large shrub from the Andes and Tierra del Fuego that is genuinely perennial to zone 6 where it dies back and resprouts reliably, and in zones 8–9 grows as a substantial, semi-evergreen shrub 5–10 feet tall producing thousands of the characteristic pendant fuchsia flowers — slender, tubular, with four flared scarlet sepals and four shorter purple petals — from early summer through fall. The profuse bloom covers the arching branches in a continuous curtain of red-and-purple miniature pendants that are irresistible to hummingbirds. In the mild, moist climate of the British Isles it has naturalized extensively in hedgerows, particularly in Ireland and western Scotland, creating dramatic late-season floral displays in the wild landscape. Several cultivars include 'Riccartonii' (smaller flowers, more compact), 'Aurea' (golden-yellow foliage), and 'Versicolor' (gray-green and cream variegated leaves).
